front: Work: in this story, the dead samurai Takehiro's ghost speaks through a medium and claims he committed suicide, contradicting six other testimonies
Work: in this story, the bandit Tajomaru claims to have lured his victim by promising cheap goods like mirrors buried under a mountain
Work: in this story, a sudden gust of wind reveals a woman's resemblance to a Bodhisattva; she later considers herself 'forsaken by Kannon'
Work: in this story, the dead woman had sold snake meat as dried fish, which the old wig-maker uses to justify robbing her hair
Work: the artist Yoshihide ('Monkey-hide') binds apprentices with chains and attacks another with a horned owl to study pained expressions
Work: Yoshihide studies a dead corpse to create Five Levels of Rebirth in this story
Work: the character Yoshihide in this story is drawn from a painter of the same name in the third scroll of the Uji Shui
Work: the thief Kandata's one act of kindness was sparing a spider; the Buddha sends a thread through a lotus pond to give him a chance to escape Hell
Work: this story adapted the 'Parable of the Onion' from The Brothers Karamazov into a tale about a thief falling back into the Pool of Blood
Work: in this semi-autobiographical story, the narrator picks up Crime and Punishment but finds it misprinted as The Brothers Karamazov
Work: in this story, the narrator learns his arsonist brother-in-law has jumped in front of a train while wearing a raincoat
Work: in this story, the priest Zenchi Naigu has his long nose boiled and stomped on to extract millet-like beads of fat; he then compares himself to Liu Bei
Work: in this novel, a kappa society cannibalizes the unemployed, practices Viverism/Lifeism, and gives fetuses the option to be born or not
Work: Patient 23 narrates this novel after falling down a hole while chasing Bag the fisherman into the land of Japanese water creatures
Work: the red-nosed Goi aspires to eat his fill of the title foodstuff but loses his appetite after his wish is fulfilled
Work: the long-nosed monk E'in erects a sign near Sarusawa Pond saying a dragon will ascend to heaven; a mass delusion results and he himself believes it
Work: this author's suicide note titled 'A Note to a Certain Old Friend' describes 'diseased nerves, lucid as ice' and recalls discussing 'Empedocles on Etna' under linden trees
work: Suicide Note / "A Note to a Certain Old Friend" / "A Fool's Life"

front: Work: this author's last work consists of 51 prose vignettes set at a bookstore and includes the aphorism 'life is not worth a single line of Baudelaire'
work: Suicide Note / "A Note to a Certain Old Friend" / "A Fool's Life"

front: Work: Kawabata quoted this author's suicide note in his Nobel lecture, alongside 'suicide is not a form of enlightenment'
work: Suicide Note / "A Note to a Certain Old Friend" / "A Fool's Life"

front: Work: the monk Lorenzo is ostracized for having a child with an umbrella-maker's daughter but is revealed to be a woman after saving the child from a fire
Work: in this story, afterlife officials mistakenly kill the protagonist (meant for Henry Barrett) and send him back with the title animal's body parts
Work: in this story, the narrator is annoyed at a lower-class girl on a train until he sees her tossing the title fruit to her waiting brothers
Author: this author had a longstanding debate with Tanizaki Jun'ichiro over the importance of plot in literature; his suicide occurred on Tanizaki's birthday
Author: in Jim Jarmusch's Ghost Dog, the title assassin steals a collection of fiction by this author during a mob hit
Author: this author's son Yasushi composed film scores including The Castle of Sand and Fires on the Plain
